Within our bodies, there are thousands of *chemical reactions* occurring which underpin all of the body’s essential processes. A chemical reaction occurs when the bonds between atoms are broken or when new bonds are formed between atoms. it is the interactions of electrons in the valence shell of atoms which form the basis of all chemical reactions. In simple terms, a chemical reaction consists of a substance or substances known as the *reactants*, which subsequently react to form the product or *products*. This is shown in Figure 3.7, which also uses the example of the reaction between hydrogen and oxygen molecules to produce water. The arrow in the equation represents the direction of the reaction, although, as will be explained later, some reactions are reversible – they can occur in both directions.

![A diagram of a chemical bond between two chlorine atoms forming a chlorine molecule. It illustrates the chemical reaction where hydrogen gas H subscript 2 reacts with oxygen gas O subscript 2 to form water H subscript 2 O. This process is an example of a chemical reaction involving energy transformation.](Attachments/Books/biochemistry-for-sport-and-exercise-maclaren/c03f007.png)

## Definition

A chemical reaction is a process in which the bonds between atoms are broken and/or new bonds are formed between atoms, transforming one or more starting substances (reactants) into one or more new substances (products). In equation form, reactants are written on the left and products on the right, separated by an arrow that indicates the direction of the reaction.

## Core Principles

- Chemical reactions are driven by the interactions of electrons in the valence shell of atoms; the breaking and forming of bonds depends on these valence-electron interactions.
- Every reaction involves reactants being converted into products, with the arrow in a chemical equation denoting the direction of conversion.
- Some reactions are reversible and can proceed in both directions (forward and reverse), while others proceed predominantly in a single direction.
- Within living organisms, thousands of such reactions occur simultaneously and collectively underpin all essential physiological processes.

## Sub-concepts

- **Reactants**: the substance or substances that enter into and are transformed by a chemical reaction.
- **Products**: the substance or substances generated as a result of the reaction.
- **Reaction direction (arrow notation)**: the symbol indicating the conversion of reactants to products; a single arrow denotes a one-way conversion, whereas reversible reactions can occur in both directions.
- **Reversible reactions**: reactions capable of proceeding in either the forward or reverse direction.
- **Example – formation of water**: hydrogen molecules (H₂) react with oxygen molecules (O₂) to produce water (H₂O), illustrating reactant-to-product conversion.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Understanding chemical reactions provides the foundation for explaining the body’s essential physiological processes, since these processes rely on thousands of concurrent chemical reactions.
- The reactant–product framework and the concept of reversibility are used to interpret biochemical equations relevant to sport and exercise contexts.
